1: Hērōdēs ho basileus ('King Herod'): this is Herod Agrippa I (grandson of Herod the Great), who ruled Judea from AD 41-44 after receiving the kingdom from Claudius. Unlike the tetrarchs of Luke's Gospel, he held the full title of king over the entire Herodian territory. His persecution of the church is politically motivated — v.3 shows he acted because it 'met with approval among the Jewish leaders' — establishing him as an opportunistic rather than principled opponent of the movement.
2: aneilen de Iakōbon ton adelphon Iōannou machaira ('he killed James the brother of John with the sword'): the death of James son of Zebedee — the first of the Twelve to die — is reported without commentary or martyrdom account. Luke's laconic treatment contrasts sharply with the extended Stephen narrative; perhaps the brevity reflects the speed and summary nature of James's execution, or a Lucan preference to focus on the continuing mission rather than deaths. Machaira (sword) suggests decapitation — the execution method for enemies of the state under Roman law.
3: hēmerai tōn azymōn ('days of Unleavened Bread'): the Passover-season arrest of Peter deliberately echoes the Passover-season arrest of Jesus (Luke 22:1-2). Luke's temporal note is not merely historical precision but typological signaling: God delivers his servant at Passover, as he has before. The Exodus pattern structures both the Passion and the apostolic mission.
4: tetradion stratiōtōn tessarsin ('four squads of four soldiers'): sixteen soldiers in rotating shifts of four meant continuous guard coverage. The military detail is historically precise; Roman custodial practice for high-value prisoners required soldiers chained directly to the prisoner (v.6). Meta to Pascha ('after the Passover'): Herod postpones the trial until after the festival, unwilling to execute during the feast — the same political calculation as before the Passion (Luke 22:2).
5: proseuchē de ēn ektenōs ginomenē ('earnest prayer was being made'): ektenōs (intensely, fervently, stretched-out) appears only twice in the NT — here and Luke 22:44, where Jesus prayed ektenōs in Gethsemane. Luke's parallel is deliberate: at the crisis point, the response is prayer stretched to its limits. The community's prayer for Peter mirrors Jesus's Gethsemane prayer before his Passion; the outcome — miraculous deliverance — is the resurrection pattern applied to the apostle.
6: hē de nyx ekeinē ēn ho Petros koimōmenos ('that very night Peter was sleeping'): Peter's sleep the night before his anticipated execution recalls the disciples' sleep in Gethsemane but inverts its significance. Where the disciples' sleep in Gethsemane represented failure of vigilance, Peter's sleep represents the peace of one entrusting himself to God (Ps 4:8: 'in peace I will lie down and sleep; for you alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety'). The chains and guards frame the setting's impossibility; the sleep frames Peter's trust.
7: angelos Kyriou epestē kai phōs elampsen en tō oikēmati ('an angel of the Lord appeared and light shone in the cell'): the angelic appearance with light is a theophanic marker throughout the OT and Luke-Acts (1:11-12; 2:9; Acts 10:3). Pataxas de tēn pleuran ('striking his side') — Luke's vocabulary is neutral but the physical gesture is intimate; the angel wakes Peter directly. The sequence — light, striking, waking, chains falling — is structured as a miniature liberation narrative.
8: The angel's instructions — dress, put on sandals, wrap your cloak — are practical and specific, in contrast to Peter's dreamlike disorientation (v.9). The mundane detail of clothing has been read as an Exodus allusion (Exod 12:11: 'eat it with your cloak tucked into your belt, your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand'), though Luke does not make the connection explicit. The specificity authenticates the account's grounding in eyewitness memory.
9: ouk ēdei hoti alēthes estin to ginomenon dia tou angelou ('he did not know that what the angel was doing was real'): Peter's cognitive state is horama-like — he processes the experience as vision rather than event. This is Luke's narrative concession to the marvel: even the recipient did not initially believe what was happening. The use of horama (vision, v.9) connects to the Acts 10 vision language; divine communication and divine action blur at their edges.
10: aute automate ēnoigē autois hē pyle ('the gate opened to them by itself'): automate (automatically, by itself) is a rare word in biblical Greek; it appears in Mark 4:28 for the earth producing grain 'by itself' as a natural process. Here the automatic opening is miraculous — no agent, no mechanism. Luke's vocabulary choice emphasizes that the gate's opening was neither accidental nor humanly engineered. The iron gate (sidarean pylēn) marks the definitive outer boundary; once through it, Peter is in the open city.
11: en emautō genomenos ('having come to himself'): the Greek idiom (en emautō ginomai) means to recover one's senses, to return to normal consciousness. It appears in Luke 15:17 for the prodigal son's moment of recognition. Peter's coming-to-himself marks the transition from dreamlike disorientation to clear-eyed recognition of divine rescue. Exeleto me ek cheiros Hērōdou ('rescued me from Herod's hand'): the OT idiom of God's hand rescuing from a king's hand (2 Sam 3:18; 1 Kgs 8:53).
12: Mary the mother of John Mark is identified as the host of a prayer meeting, suggesting a house-church gathering. The name-chain — Mary, John called Mark — introduces John Mark, who will become significant as a point of contention between Paul and Barnabas (15:37-39) and who is identified with the author of the second Gospel in later tradition (Papias, ca. AD 130). Luke's attention to names in Acts reflects careful sourcing; his information about the Jerusalem community is consistently specific.
13: Rhodē (Rhoda, meaning 'rose'): a maidservant who is named — unusual for slaves in ancient texts. The naming signals that Rhoda's role in the story was remembered in community tradition. Luke's narrative here is unusually novelistic — the servant's astonishment, her running back without opening the door, her excited announcement — suggesting a story told and retold within the community with affection for its vivid details.
14: Rhoda's failure to open the door from sheer joy (apo tēs charas) is Luke's most humanly comic scene in Acts. She recognizes Peter's voice before seeing him, races back to report, and leaves him knocking at the gate — a detail that the community apparently told for years. Luke's inclusion of this human particularity suggests that his sources included firsthand testimony from participants; it is precisely the kind of detail that survives in oral tradition because of its combination of joy and absurdity.
15: ho angelos autou estin ('it is his angel'): the community's explanation — that it must be Peter's guardian angel — reflects the first-century Jewish tradition of personal angels modeled on Dan 10:13-21 and the OT divine-messenger tradition. They prayed for Peter but do not immediately believe he has been delivered; they posit that the angel they associate with Peter has taken his form. Luke records the belief without endorsing or dismissing it; the narrative's irony is that the actual Peter is outside knocking while they debate his angelic substitute.
16: exestēsan ('they were astonished/beside themselves'): Luke's characteristic vocabulary for the response to miraculous divine action (2:7; 8:13; 9:21; 10:45). The astonishment of those who have been praying earnestly for Peter's release is Luke's quiet theological comment: prayer is offered in faith, but the faith of those praying is itself often smaller than the God to whom they pray. The community's astonishment is not a failure but a measure of the miracle's magnitude.
17: kataseisas autois tē cheiri sigān ('motioning to them with his hand to be quiet'): the oratorial gesture — right hand raised, palm forward — is the same as Paul's gesture in 13:16 and 21:40. It is the standard ancient rhetorical signal for attention and silence before speaking. Iakōbō kai tois adelphois ('to James and the brothers'): this is James the Lord's brother (Gal 1:19), who is emerging as the leader of the Jerusalem church. The instruction to report to him signals the authority structure Luke is tracking toward the Jerusalem Council of ch.15.
18: tarache ouk oligē en tois stratiōtais ('no small commotion among the soldiers'): Luke's characteristic litotes — 'not small' = considerable — understates the military crisis. Ti ara ho Petros egeneto ('what had become of Peter'): the question is unanswerable within the framework available to the soldiers; their prisoner is gone from a locked, guarded cell. The commotion is the mirror image of the community's prayer meeting — there, joy and astonishment; here, confusion and crisis.
19: anakrinas ('having cross-examined'): the legal term for thorough judicial interrogation. Herod's response to failing to find Peter is to execute the guards — standard Roman military discipline for soldiers who allowed a prisoner to escape (cf. 16:27, where the Philippian jailer assumes the same fate and considers suicide). The detail authenticates Luke's knowledge of Roman military practice. Katēlthen apo tēs Ioudaias eis Kaisareian: Herod's withdrawal to Caesarea sets up his fatal speech in vv.21-23.
20: thymomachōn ('extremely angry'): a hapax in the NT; the compound — thymos (passion/rage) + machomai (fight) — describes violent, passionate anger. Herod's quarrel with Tyre and Sidon is an economic dispute (they depended on Herod's territory for food imports, v.20b). The deputation's mission — securing audience through Blastus the chamberlain — uses the standard vocabulary of Hellenistic diplomacy. Luke's knowledge of this internal dynastic and commercial detail suggests he had access to Herodian court sources.
21: endysas esthēta basilikēn ('dressed in royal robes'): Josephus's parallel account (Antiquities 19.8.2) describes silver robes that gleamed brilliantly in the morning sun, causing the crowd's exclamation. Luke's account and Josephus's are independent corroborations of the same event, each preserving different details. Dēmēgorein ('delivered a public address') is a technical term for formal civic oratory; Herod's speech is a set-piece of Hellenistic royal self-presentation.
22: theou phōnē kai ouk anthrōpou ('the voice of a god, not of a man'): the crowd's acclamation is the precise form of blasphemy — attributing divine status to a mortal. Josephus adds that Herod saw an owl and took it as an omen of death (recalling an earlier omen), but Luke focuses on the theological issue: Herod accepted acclamation that should be given to God alone. The structural echo of Isa 14:13-15 ('I will make myself like the Most High') and Ezek 28:2 is the theological framework Luke invokes without citing.
23: parachrēma de epataxen auton angelos Kyriou, anth' hōn ouk edōken tēn doxan tō theō ('immediately an angel of the Lord struck him, because he did not give glory to God'): the causation is explicit — anth' hōn ('because') names the theological reason. skōlēkobrōtos genomenos ('becoming eaten by worms'): a distinctive vocabulary item (hapax in NT) that Josephus's account corroborates (violent intestinal pain, five-day illness, death at Caesarea). The divine judgment is immediate, precise, and publicly witnessed — the inverse of the community's miraculous deliverance in vv.7-11.
24: ho de logos tou theou ēuxanen kai eplēthyneto ('the word of God kept growing and multiplying'): the fourth of Luke's Acts growth-summaries (2:47; 4:4; 5:14; 6:7; 12:24). Luke places it immediately after Herod's death — the persecutor is gone; the word continues. The pairing of auxanō (grow) and plēthynō (multiply) echoes the language of Israel's growth in Egypt (Exod 1:7 LXX: auxanon kai plēthynomenon), framing the church's expansion as the continuation of God's people-multiplying work.
25: hypostrepsan ex Ierousalēm ('having returned from Jerusalem'): the reference to the relief mission of 11:29-30; Barnabas and Saul had completed the famine-relief delivery and now return to Antioch with John Mark. This transitional verse closes the Jerusalem section (chs.11-12) and opens the Antioch mission section (ch.13). Luke's structure moves the narrative's center of gravity from Jerusalem to Antioch, as the Gentile mission is about to begin.
